title: What Is Grip Strength Testing and Why Does It Matter?
description: Grip strength testing measures the maximal force your hand can exert. It is a validated proxy for whole-body strength and a strong predictor of falls, frailty and all-cause mortality.
author: Able Care Clinical Team
excerpt: Grip strength is one of the most studied biomarkers in geriatric medicine. It predicts falls, frailty, hospitalization and mortality. Here is what clinicians and operators need to know about grip strength testing, how it works and why it matters for falls prevention.
content:
What is grip strength?

Grip strength is the maximal force your hand can exert when squeezing a dynamometer. It is a validated proxy for whole-body muscle strength and one of the most reliable predictors of health outcomes in adults over 60.

Unlike many clinical measurements, grip strength is quick to capture (under 30 seconds), non-invasive and requires minimal patient cooperation. That makes it uniquely suited to screening at scale.

Why does grip strength matter?

The evidence base for grip strength is extensive. The Lancet published a landmark study in 2015 showing that grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure. Since then, research has linked low grip strength to increased risk of falls, frailty, sarcopenia, cardiovascular disease, respiratory disease, renal disease and cancer mortality.

For falls prevention specifically, grip strength is a proxy for upper-body strength, which correlates strongly with lower-body strength and overall functional capacity. A person with declining grip strength is more likely to fall, more likely to be hospitalized after a fall and less likely to recover independently.

How is grip strength measured?

Traditionally, grip strength is measured using a Jamar hydraulic dynamometer. The patient squeezes the device three times with each hand, and the highest reading is recorded. This approach has been the clinical standard for decades, but it has significant limitations: no digital data capture, no longitudinal tracking, no normative comparison and no integration with care plans or EHR systems.

Digital grip strength platforms like GripAble capture the same clinical-grade measurement with Bluetooth-connected sensors. The data is recorded automatically, compared against age and sex-matched normative data and integrated into a clinical workflow. This is the approach used by Able Assess.

What is a normal grip strength?

Normal grip strength varies by age, sex and dominant hand. As a general guide, the European Working Group on Sarcopenia in Older People (EWGSOP2) defines low grip strength as below 27 kg for men and below 16 kg for women. Values below these thresholds are associated with significantly increased risk of falls, functional decline and mortality.

Grip strength in falls prevention

Grip strength is one of four metrics captured by Able Assess, alongside sit-to-stand, gait speed and Timed Up and Go. Together, these four measurements provide a multifactorial picture of falls risk that is more predictive than any single test.

The NICE 2025 falls prevention guidelines recommend objective, standardized screening using validated tools. Grip strength measurement using a digital dynamometer meets this standard and provides the data trail that operators, payers and regulators increasingly require.

Key takeaways

  • Grip strength is a validated proxy for whole-body strength and a strong predictor of falls, frailty and mortality.
  • The Lancet (2015) found grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than blood pressure.
  • Digital grip dynamometers capture clinical-grade data with automatic normative comparison and longitudinal tracking.
  • Grip strength is one of four metrics in the Able Assess workflow, providing a multifactorial picture of falls risk.
  • NICE 2025 guidelines recommend objective, standardized falls screening using validated tools.
